summary:
Lepenski Vir, situated on the banks of the Danube in present-day Serbia at the Iron Gates gorge, was occupied from approximately 6200 to 4500 BC and represents a remarkable example of a complex semi-sedentary community predating full agricultural adoption. The site contains the remains of trapezoidal stone-floored buildings arranged in a systematic layout around a central hearth, an architectural regularity unusual for this era. Most extraordinary are the large sandstone sculptures found in and around the hearths — naturalistic yet stylized human and fish-like faces that are among the earliest monumental sculptures in European prehistory.

Lepenski Vir's inhabitants relied heavily on fishing in the productive Danube rapids, supplementing their diet with hunting and foraging. Over time, domesticated animals and cultivated plants appear in the record, reflecting the gradual absorption of Neolithic practices from neighboring farming communities. The site illustrates that social complexity, ritual life, and sedentary architecture were not exclusively products of agriculture, and that the transition to farming in southeastern Europe was a drawn-out, mosaic process rather than a sudden replacement of one lifestyle by another.
